column13: Definition of arithmetic, mediaeval classification of number, (with the system of ratios by Boethius and his followers). The second part of the work contains a short treatment of algorism, including the fundamental operations and roots. The third tractatus relates to common fractions and the fourth to physical or sexagesimal fractions. The arithmetic closes with a treatment of line reckoning, giving the four fundamental operations and the rule of three.

column13: The manual (10 fols) has the typical structure of an abacus treatise. It contains a brief treatment of the 'species ' (the fundamental operations) with integers, omitting ' duplatio ' and ' mediatio ' but including progressions and roots. This is followed by a discussion of common fractions ('Algorifmus nouus de//minutijs vulgaribus '), a single page on sexagesimal fractions ('de minutijs Phificalibus '), and a page on proportion. It is one of the first books to identify proportion with the rule of three.

column13: [from Taming the unknown, p. 205]: drawing directly from the work of Pacioli and the maestri dšabbaco, the Portuguese mathematician Pedro Nu¢es (1502ą1578), began work on the manuscript he would eventually publish as the Libro de algebra (or Book on Algebra) (1567). Both as Royal Cosmographer under King John III (the Pius) of Portugal and as professor of mathematics at the University of Coimbra, Nu¢es gave instruction in the art of navigation to those associated with Portugalšs merchant and naval fleets. His Libro de algebra provided the mathematical underpinnings of that instructiončand much morečadopting Paciolišs abbreviated notational style and treating the solution not only of linear and quadratic equations but also that of a cubic equation of the type x3 +cx = d following the spectacular mid-sixteenth-century work of the Italians Niccolś Tartaglia and Girolamo Cardano

column13: Definition of arithmetic, mediaeval classification of number, (with the system of ratios by Boethius and his followers). The second part of the work contains a short treatment of algorism, including the fundamental operations and roots. The third tractatus relates to common fractions and the fourth to physical or sexagesimal fractions. The arithmetic closes with a treatment of line reckoning, giving the four fundamental operations and the rule of three.

column13: The work is divided into 12 books. Book 1 deals 'von der Venetianifchen oder Kauffmennifchen Practic' (what is usually called 'Welsch practice' by Rechenmeisters). It is essentially a collection of problems. Book 2: denominate numbers, rule of three. Book 3: wheights. Book 4: problems for cashiers, exchange. Book 5: practical problems (purchase). Book 6: inverse and compound proportion. The other books present misc. business problems.

column13: Very comprehensive work. Part I: numeration, operations, progressions, reckoning table (tokens and feathers), fractions, operations with fractions. Part II ('practica'): rule of three, exchange, tare, fusti, money exchange, profit and loss, interest rates (with a very strange name: 'Juden Rechnung'), currencies in different marketplaces, companies, factors, Stich rechnung, alloying, misc problems, rule of false. Third part: roots, operations with roots, algebra. Part IV: bookkeeping, with examples of different kinds of accounting books.
